title: Ready, Steady, Go! Game Zone
source: Generated from approved unit runtime and linked master dictionary

games.title: Meaning Match
games.description: Match each unit word to its approved child-friendly meaning.

rounds.prompt: Which word means: The top part of your body, with your eyes, ears, nose and mouth.
rounds.explanation: head: The top part of your body, with your eyes, ears, nose and mouth.

rounds.prompt: Which word means: To jump up and down on one foot.
rounds.explanation: hop: To jump up and down on one foot.

rounds.prompt: Which word means: The clear drink that your body needs every single day.
rounds.explanation: water: The clear drink that your body needs every single day.

games.title: Spelling Builder
games.description: Choose letter tiles to build important unit words.

rounds.prompt: Build the word that matches the clue.
rounds.clue: The long part of your body from your shoulder to your hand.

rounds.prompt: Build the word that matches the clue.
rounds.clue: To push yourself off the ground with both feet.

rounds.prompt: Build the word that matches the clue.
rounds.clue: To rest with your eyes closed, usually at night.

games.title: Sentence Puzzle
games.description: Put unit words into the correct sentence order.

rounds.prompt: Build the complete model sentence.
rounds.answer: I wear a sun hat on my head outside.

rounds.prompt: Build the complete model sentence.
rounds.answer: I hold my pencil with my fingers.

rounds.prompt: Build the complete model sentence.
rounds.answer: The children hop like little rabbits in the garden.

games.title: Language Choice
games.description: Choose the form that makes the unit language accurate.

rounds.prompt: Which of these helps your body stay healthy and strong?
rounds.explanation: Water helps your body work well. The other three choices keep your body still or tired.

rounds.prompt: In the poem “Reach for the Sky!”, where do you put your finger?
rounds.explanation: Line three of the poem says, “Put your finger on your nose.” The other body parts are not in the poem.

rounds.prompt: In “The Big Race”, why did Theo fall over?
choices: He was waving to the crowd and did not watch his feet.
He tripped over a big bag that somebody had left on the grass.
He stopped in the middle of the race to drink his water.
He was helping Amal to stand up again after her fall.
rounds.answer: He was waving to the crowd and did not watch his feet.
rounds.explanation: The story says Theo was so busy waving that he did not look at his feet, and then he tripped.

games.title: Reading Detective
games.description: Use unit evidence to solve each reading clue.

rounds.prompt: Which line of the poem is said at the beginning and then again at the end?
rounds.explanation: Review the unit text evidence: “Reach for the sky!”

rounds.prompt: What is the special day at school called in this listening text?
choices: He was waving to the crowd, so he did not look at his feet.
rounds.explanation: Review the unit text evidence: “Get Up and Move” day.

rounds.prompt: Why did Theo trip and fall in the relay race?
choices: She walked back and helped Theo stand up. Accept any reason that uses the story, such as Theo looking sad or rubbing his hurt...
He was waving to the crowd, so he did not look at his feet.
rounds.answer: He was waving to the crowd, so he did not look at his feet.
rounds.explanation: Review the unit text evidence: He was waving to the crowd, so he did not look at his feet.

games.description: Arrange unit language in a clear, accurate order.

rounds.prompt: Put this unit sentence in order.

rounds.prompt: Put this unit sentence in order.
rounds.answer: I drink water when I feel thirsty.

rounds.prompt: Put this unit sentence in order.

games.title: Definition Dash
games.description: Choose the approved meaning before moving to the next clue.

rounds.prompt: What does 'clap' mean in this unit?
choices: To hit your two hands together to make a sound.
The top part of your body, with your eyes, ears, nose and mouth.
The long part of your body from your shoulder to your hand.
rounds.answer: To hit your two hands together to make a sound.
rounds.explanation: clap: To hit your two hands together to make a sound.

rounds.prompt: What does 'energy' mean in this unit?
choices: The top part of your body, with your eyes, ears, nose and mouth.
The long part of your body from your shoulder to your hand.
The power you get from food and rest to play and learn.
rounds.answer: The power you get from food and rest to play and learn.
rounds.explanation: energy: The power you get from food and rest to play and learn.

rounds.prompt: What does 'arm' mean in this unit?
choices: One of the five long parts at the end of your hand.
The long part of your body from your shoulder to your hand.
The top part of your body, with your eyes, ears, nose and mouth.
rounds.answer: The long part of your body from your shoulder to your hand.
rounds.explanation: arm: The long part of your body from your shoulder to your hand.

games.title: Word Type Power
games.description: Identify how each vocabulary word works in English.

rounds.prompt: What type of word is 'healthy'?
rounds.explanation: healthy is used as a adjective in this dictionary sense.

rounds.prompt: What type of word is 'reach'?
rounds.explanation: reach is used as a verb in this dictionary sense.

rounds.prompt: What type of word is 'toe'?
rounds.explanation: toe is used as a noun in this dictionary sense.

games.title: Memory Pairs
games.description: Reveal tiles and connect each word with its meaning.

rounds.prompt: Match vocabulary set 1.
pairs: The part at the end of your arm, with a thumb and fingers.
The front part of your body below your chest, also called the stomach.
To jump up and down on one foot.

rounds.prompt: Match vocabulary set 2.
pairs: To hit your two hands together to make a sound.
Moving your body on purpose to keep it fit and strong.
Having lots of power in your body or your muscles.

rounds.prompt: Match vocabulary set 3.
pairs: The power you get from food and rest to play and learn.
To move your body around so that you face another way.
To move your arms up and down like the wings of a bird.

games.title: Question Quest
games.description: Read each unit question carefully and choose the best answer.

rounds.prompt: Which sentence uses the present continuous correctly?
choices: She are jumping on the grass.
She am jumping on the grass.
She is jumping on the grass.
rounds.answer: She is jumping on the grass.
rounds.explanation: She takes the helping word is. Am belongs with I, and are belongs with you, we and they.

rounds.prompt: Which is the correct -ing form of the verb hop?
rounds.explanation: Hop is a short word with one vowel, so the p doubles before -ing. Hoping comes from hope, and hopped is a past form.

rounds.prompt: Which of these sentences is a command?
rounds.explanation: A command starts with the action word and tells someone to do something. The others describe, ask or report.

games.title: Unit Mission
games.description: Finish with a mixed challenge across the whole unit.

rounds.prompt: Which word means the part at the end of your arm, with fingers?
rounds.explanation: A hand sits at the end of each arm. Water, sleep and energy are health words, not body parts.

rounds.prompt: Sami holds his pencil with his ____s.
rounds.explanation: We hold a pencil between our fingers and thumb. Toes are on your feet, and water and turn are not body parts of the hand.

rounds.prompt: Which word means to jump up and down on one foot?
rounds.explanation: Hopping uses one foot only. Clapping uses hands, water is a drink, and head is a body part.
